inv03.lastWrong: No sooner had they eaten when the ceiling crashed.

tf_42.lastWrong: not only had the board rejected

pp_pr04.lastWrong: We lost two team members last month.

aph32.lastWrong: We are at the stage where costs must be cut.

inv04.lastWrong: Hardly had we arrived than the problems started.

art19.lastWrong: We are pleased to announce the appointment of Director of Operations.

aph18.lastWrong: In a wake of the merger several departments were restructured.

pf01.lastWrong: After you built trust, it became very easy to chat.

weak_patterns: article 'the' overuse with abstract / zero-article nouns (at the stage, take the action)

phrasal verb production — 'get across' cold-production gap (recognition with options clean, cold transform fails: produced 'get about' 2026-05-03). 'get around to' stickiest particle, 3rd consecutive session in recognition; produces cleanly after one same-session exposure. Latinate-causal PV separability with long NPs (bring about, give rise to) — tendency to separate where adjacent is natural.

opposite/contrast preposition: 'opposite of' (not 'opposite to') for nouns; 'unlike / in contrast to / as opposed to' for clauses. Recurred 2x in 2026-05-03 free_write session. Candidate for focused 5-6 item error_correction drill (per the PV preposition drill model from 2026-05-02).
coach_notes.engagement_notes: Prefers claude.ai chat pacing for live exercise sessions — CC felt slower than chat (2026-04-30 feedback). For Step 6 persistence, always show human-readable bullets (not raw JSON) before writing. PV training ladder: recognition (Smart-mode quiz filtered to PV) → particle_sort → transform/free_write. Don't start cold on PV transform without a recognition warm-up in the same session or the day before. Two-part format (8-10 particle_sort + 4-5 transform same session) validated 2026-05-02 — combined 73-96%, recognition→production within single session works. Use this format for future PV sessions. Prefers concise explanations; pushes back on verbose responses. Next: in two-stage sessions, use **different contexts/sentences in recognition vs production** — same PVs, different stems, to avoid carry-over that masks true production gaps.
strong_patterns: PV preposition pairings mastered 2026-05-02 (6/6 error_correction drill after two-stage session isolation)

recent_observations.note: Stats sweep across 2723 answers / 125 sessions. Phrasal verb production gap confirmed across 6+ stuck input items. Articles 74% over 234 attempts — stable fossilised.
recent_observations.author: claude_code
claude_code
recent_observations.note: Article drill 8/10 (87%, vs 74% baseline). All 3 misses clustered on fossilised over-defining pattern: 'take an action', 'behind the schedule' (item 2); missed 'at the early stage' → 'an early stage' (item 7). Definite reference and zero with abstract uncountables clean. Next session: Russian Trap micro-drill on fixed expressions + 'на ранней стадии' anti-calque.

recent_observations.author: claude_code
recent_observations.note: PV Family Drill 5/8 (63%, or 50% strict). GET-family particle production confirmed weakest — 0/3 strict on across/down to/around to. BRING/TAKE missed abstract/causal particles (about, over). Verb-form slip after modal ('will took'). Player self-diagnosis: production drills feel too steep cold — wants recognition layer first. Confirms input-type 0% from stats sweep is real, not UI artefact. Second session today, ran after article_drill 8/10.

Two-stage PV session: particle_sort 8/10 + transform 3/5 (combined 73%, vs 63% last PV drill). Recognition→production ladder validated — production less brittle than cold-start. 'get around to' confirmed stickiest particle (missed in both halves; same item structure tripped him twice). Tense drift under particle load persists ('finally get across' for past tense, family of 'will took' from 04-30). 'take over' needs 'from' before person — Russian direct-object trap (заменит Дэвида → take over from David). Player feedback: good, quite tough to remember.
recent_observations.author: claude_code

recent_observations.note: Two-stage easy-PV session: particle_sort 8/8 + transform 3.5/4 (combined 95.5%, vs 73% previous PV session). Transparent semantics (look up, find out, call off, give up) → clean production. Preposition pairings sticky: 'give up on' (not 'with'), 'take over from' (not direct object). Next: dedicated 5-item 'PV + preposition' micro-drill. Player feedback: easy. CC note: next session avoid repeating phrases between recognition and production phases.
recent_observations.author: claude_code

recent_observations.note: Focused 6-item error_correction on PV preposition pairings: 6/6. Pattern isolated after two-stage easy-PV session showed '3.5/4' due to preposition (give up with → on). Focused drill confirms: boundaries now explicit. Pattern solved via explicit isolation + error-correction. Player feedback: good.
recent_observations.author: claude_code

recent_observations.note: Free_write production solid (4/5 strict). Phrasal verbs natural under stress (work around, take on, dig into, bring about — all 4 correct). Article weak spot confirmed: definite/indefinite error with titles/roles ('a team leader' → 'the team leader'). Collocation awareness emerging (bring about + concrete outcome awkward; deliver/come up with more natural). Next: articles + titles micro-drill.
recent_observations.author: claude_code

recent_observations.note: PV two-stage 9.5/12 (79%, hard tier — vs 73% hard 05-02). New 'different stems' rule (engagement_notes) surfaced production gap on 'get across': clean recognition with options, failed cold transform ('get about'). 'get around to' recognition still soft 3rd session running but produced cleanly after one same-session exposure. Tense drift under particle load resolved this session — 'took over from David' (past) and 'will bring … about' (modal+base) clean, no slips. RU direct-object trap on 'take over from' produced unprompted. New finding: latinate-causal PV separability with long NPs (bring about a step-change vs *bring a step-change about). Player feedback: stems felt over-corporate, pitch-deck register, unlikely to use in real work — author next session from operational/hallway register.
recent_observations.author: claude_code

recent_observations.note: Free Write 60 min on bike + Turkey trip. Articles full repertoire today: missing 'the' with superlatives, 'a' added to uncountable (scenery), 'the'-overuse with generic plural (the rest days), missing 'a' with count noun (a tourist town) — both directions of the fossilised fault in one session. NEW recurring pattern: opposite/contrast preposition error TWICE in same session ('opposite to Bahrain'; 'in the opposite to Fethiye') — candidate for focused error_correction drill. RU L1 calques: 'recover myself' (reflexive), 'touristic', 'as early as possible', 'across the coast', 'up to 11 am' for clock-time. PV slip 'blended in into' (redundant + wrong particle). Same-session uptake on 'along the coast' (self-corrected mid-session after earlier fix). Self-driven grammar question on until/till/by/to/up to — handled mini-drill cleanly including the trap item. Pushed back on framing twice. Tense drift in past narrative continues (slips to present mid-story).
recent_observations.author: claude_code

recent_observations.note: Focused error_correction drill on opposite/contrast preposition (follow-up to same-day free_write artem_fw_1777831978886_ky13). 2/6 strict (33%), 3/6 with retry. Below PV preposition drill 2026-05-02 (6/6) — this pattern more entrenched. Two sub-patterns: (1) opposite/opposed lexical swap (items 1, 3 original) — over-suggestion in item 1 feedback ('opposed to' as alternative) may have primed item 3 repeat; cleared after explicit lexeme split. (2) 'in the opposite to/of' structural retention (items 2, 5) — got the 'of' swap but leading 'in the' survived twice, even after item 2 teaching. Same-session uptake on predicate 'the complete opposite of' (item 3 retry clean, item 6 clean). Decoy 'unlike + noun' recognised cleanly (item 4 fastest at ~27s). Recommend: retest in 2-3 sessions, focus exclusively on dropping leading 'in the'. Lesson for coach: avoid offering 'opposed to' as alternative in initial teaching — semantic distinction (active opposition vs mere difference) needs explicit separation upfront.
recent_observations.author: claude_code

'last' vs 'latest' word choice

'a support van' → 'the support van' (shared knowledge)
'a Wednesday' → zero article
'a lunch stop' → 'the lunch stop'

recovery after completion needs get over, not get through

meta.note: Player requested easy session. Clean 8/8 on common GET/TAKE particle recognition.

errors: Item 2: 'take an action' / 'fall behind the schedule' should be 'take action' / 'behind schedule' (zero-article fixed expressions)
Item 7: missed 'at the early stage' — should be 'at an early stage' (first mention + adjective + countable singular = indefinite)
exercises.topic: Articles Decision Drill — abstract/zero-article + first-mention indefinite (business/O&G)

meta.miss_cluster: all 3 misses on the same fossilised L1 pattern: over-defining where English wants zero/indefinite

errors: Item 1: word order + redundant 'understood' after 'get across' (keyword conceded — full PV leaked from initial prompt version)
Item 2: 'brought up' for causation → should be 'brought about' (raise-topic vs cause confusion)
Item 4: 'got on' (incomplete/wrong) for 'started seriously working' → should be 'got down to'
Item 6: 'will took on the team' → 'will take over the team' (verb form after modal + particle: take on = accept new vs take over = replace)
Item 7: 'get it over' (incomplete) for 'finally handle' → should be 'get around to' (preferred) or 'get it over with'

meta.player_feedback: Difficult but useful. Wants easier PV recognition / quiz first before production drills.

errors: item 3: chose 'through to' for 'haven't got ___ reviewing the invoice' — correct: 'around to' (reaching person vs finally doing distinction)
item 10: chose 'up' for 'I'll take ___ the FP&A workstream while Ahmed is on leave' — correct: 'on' (start hobby vs accept duty distinction)
item 1: 'I finally get across to the board' — particle correct, tense wrong (should be 'got'). Half credit. Same family as 'will took' from last PV drill.
item 4: 'Maria will take over David as Head of Risk' — missing 'from' before person ('take over from David'). Russian L1 direct-object trap. Half credit.
item 5: 'haven't gotten on with a reply' — wrong particle, should be 'got around to replying'. Same 'around to' miss as item 3 of particle_sort. Stickiest particle for Artem right now.

meta.scoring_note: half credit for particle-correct/form-wrong; combined with full marks: 3/5

errors: item 4: 'gave up trying with the deal' — particle correct (up), preposition wrong ('on' not 'with'). 'give up on' is the fixed pairing. Half credit. Preposition-pairing pattern confirmed: both give-up/on and take-over/from have caught him.

exercises.topic: PV preposition pairings (give up on / take over from / come up with / get on with / run out of / look after)

meta.note: Strong production under stress. PVs natural. Article focus on definite/indefinite with titles/roles.

errors: Item 1: Missing article 'the' before Gulf (lowercase stylistic choice noted, not grammar error)
Item 3: 'a team leader' should be 'the team leader' (definite reference error)
Item 4: 'bring about a quick fix' — awkward collocation (more natural: deliver/come up with)
Item 2 (recognition): 'get down to' for postponement frame → expected 'get around to' (3rd consecutive session miss on this particle)
Item 9 (transform): produced 'get about' → expected 'get across'; cold production failed despite clean Stage 1 recognition

Item 11 (transform): 'bring a step-change about in unit costs' → expected 'bring about a step-change in unit costs'; PV+modal correct, separability with long NP wrong (latinate-causal PVs resist separation with full noun phrases)

items.submitted_answer: Eventually, I managed to get about my concern about the timeline to the project sponsor.
It took us six months to get around to the legacy systems audit.
The new operating model will bring a step-change about in unit costs across upstream.
Last quarter, Sarah took over from David as head of trading.

exercises.topic: PV ladder — GET-family + BRING/TAKE abstract particles (two-stage: particle recognition + transform production, different stems)

items.submitted_answer: The contractor's approach is totally opposed of ours.
items.stem: The contractor's approach is totally opposite to ours.
items.expected_answer: The contractor's approach is totally the opposite of ours.
items.notes: Mixed lexemes: opposed (which takes 'to') + of (which goes with 'opposite'). Over-suggestion in feedback ('opposed to' as alternative) may have primed item 3 same error.

items.expected_answer: Unlike last quarter, drilling is on schedule. (or: In contrast to / As opposed to / Contrary to)
items.submitted_answer: In the opposite of last quarter, drilling is on schedule.
items.stem: In the opposite to last quarter, drilling is on schedule.
items.notes: Got the 'of' swap but kept the leading 'in the opposite' structure. The phrase isn't standard English — likely literal map of RU 'в противоположность'.

items.expected_answer: Climbing in Alanya felt completely different from riding in Bahrain. (or: was the complete opposite of)
items.stem: Climbing in Alanya felt completely opposite to riding in Bahrain.
items.submitted_answer: Climbing in Alanya felt completely opposed to riding in Bahrain.
items.notes: Second occurrence of opposite/opposed swap. After explicit lexical reset, retry produced 'the complete opposite of' cleanly (~187s). Original score stands.

items.stem: Unlike Fethiye, Alanya is full of cruise-ship tourists.
items.notes: Decoy recognised cleanly. Unlike + noun, no preposition — handled fast.
Same as item 2 — fixed 'to' to 'of' but retained leading 'in the opposite'. Pattern persists across two items in same drill despite item 2 explanation.
items.submitted_answer: Davis's view on the maintenance shutdown is in the opposite of mine.
items.stem: Davis's view on the maintenance shutdown is in the opposite to mine.
items.expected_answer: Davis's view on the maintenance shutdown is the opposite of mine. (or: in contrast to / contrasts with / differs from)

items.stem: The crowds in Alanya were the complete opposite to what we expected.
items.submitted_answer: The crowds in Alanya were the complete opposite of what we expected.
items.expected_answer: The crowds in Alanya were the complete opposite of what we expected.
items.notes: Clean 'the complete opposite of' production — same pattern as item 3 retry. Predicate-noun structure landed by end of session.

exercises.topic: opposite/contrast preposition focused drill (follow-up to free_write artem_fw_1777831978886_ky13)
exercises.session_summary: Focused error_correction drill on opposite/contrast preposition, follow-up to same-day free_write. 2/6 strict (33%), 3/6 with retry. Below PV preposition drill 2026-05-02 (6/6) — pattern more entrenched. Two distinct sub-patterns: (1) opposite/opposed lexical swap, cleared after explicit split; (2) 'in the opposite to/of' structural retention, persisted across items 2 and 5 even after teaching. Same-session uptake on predicate 'the complete opposite of' (items 3 retry, 6).

coach_sessions.session_summary: Free Write on bike + Turkey trip (Fethiye gravel, Alanya cycling camp). Productive session hitting the full articles repertoire (missing 'the' with superlatives, 'a' added to uncountables, 'the'-overuse with generic plurals, missing 'a' with count nouns) plus a recurring 'opposite/contrast preposition' error TWICE in the same session ('opposite to Bahrain' and 'in the opposite to Fethiye'). Multiple RU L1 calques (recover myself, touristic, as early as possible, across the coast, up to 11 am for clock-time). PV preposition slip on 'blended in into'. Tense drift in past narrative. Self-driven grammar question on until/till/by/to/up to — handled mini-drill cleanly including identifying the trap item. Same-session uptake on 'along the coast' (corrected mid-session after earlier 'across the coast' fix). Pushed back on framing twice (work-ride structure, autocorrect). Strong wins: 'despite the heat' (no 'of'), 'took turns', 'fellow riders', 'vibrant', 'due to', uncountable 'weather' handled cleanly even when 'scenery' tripped. Next focus: 'opposite of / in contrast to / unlike' error_correction drill (recurred twice, prime candidate for explicit isolation like the PV preposition drill on 2026-05-02).
error_patterns_observed: articles: missing 'the' with superlatives ('the best gravel routes')
articles: 'a' added to uncountable ('a scenery'); zero needed
articles: 'the'-overuse with generic plural ('the rest days') — fossilised pattern
articles: missing 'a' with count noun ('a tourist town')
opposite/contrast preposition — RECURRED twice in same session: 'opposite to Bahrain' → 'opposite of'; 'in the opposite to Fethiye' → 'unlike / in contrast to'
PV preposition pairing: 'blended in into' → 'blended in with / blended into'
RU L1 calques: 'recover myself' (reflexive), 'touristic' (calque), 'as early as possible' (literal), 'across the coast' (preposition), 'up to 11 am' (clock-time)
RU L1 word order: 'We were for 2 weeks in Fethiye' → 'We were in Fethiye for 2 weeks' (place before duration)
tense drift in past narrative — multiple present-tense slips ('we have', 'we spend', 'I have to recover')
until/till/by/to/up to confusion — addressed via mini-drill mid-session
time clause: 'The next day I arrived' → 'The day after I arrived'
word choice: 'met my old friends' (first-time vs reunion); 'weak guys' (harsher in EN than RU)

messages.content: Hey Artem — ready when you are. What's on your mind today? Options: work week, F1 (Miami coming up), bike (got out at all over the weekend?).
bike. just finished an outdoor ride
Nice. How's the heat? May in Bahrain is right on the edge of rideable outdoors. Tell me about the ride — distance, pace, how you felt at the end.
Today was hotter than usual. A lack of wind really heated up my ride. Two days ago was very windy, and I don't have a problem to ride up to 11 am

Two fixes: 'have a problem to ride' → 'have a problem riding' (gerund after have a problem). 'up to 11 am' → 'until 11 am' (until for time endpoints; up to is for limits/quantities). 'A lack of wind heated up my ride' is understandable but slightly off — 'With no wind, the ride felt much hotter' is more natural. ✓ 'A lack of wind' clean indefinite article.
расскажи побольше я часто вижу исправления until till to

[Detailed explanation: until=till (interchangeable, till informal); by=deadline (no later than); to=clock fragments / X-to-Y ranges; up to=quantities. Russian до maps differently. Mini drill: 1) 'send numbers to Thursday' 2) 'I'll be at office till 6' 3) 'wait up to 30 minutes'.]
1 by, 2 until, 3 to is OK. why till is wrong in 2? you said until and till is same

Sharp pushback — partly right. Item 2 was a trap, already correct (till=until both work). Your 'until' also fine, just don't think 'till' was wrong. Item 3 'up to 30 minutes' correct because 30 minutes = quantity/duration (max amount), different from clock-time '11 am' which needs until.
back to bike
When still and hot, how do you cope? How long was today's ride and where?
I tried to ride slower. Anyway I rode today with a bunch of guys from work. They are usually pretty slow. On the way back I pedaled hard, despite the heat I wanted to get home as early as possible.

Main correction: 'as early as possible' → 'as soon as possible' / 'as quickly as possible' (early=time of day; soon=minimal delay; Russian как можно раньше maps to as soon as possible here). Comma after 'despite the heat'. ✓ 'a bunch of guys from work', 'despite the heat' (no 'of'), 'on the way back'.
Come on. Two weak guys rode a shorter loop. We rode fast after they got to their cars.
Misread the structure — got it. ✓ Clean turn. Optional upgrade: 'two weak guys' → 'two slower lads', 'tapped out / peeled off / called it early'. 'Weak' in English is harsher than RU 'слабые'.
35 km/h on the way back, which is about 3-4 km/h faster than my solo rides. It was just the two of us, of course, we took turns. He's faster since I'm still acclimatizing from much chiller Turkey.

'much chiller Turkey' → 'chillier' (chill is noun/verb/slang, chilly is the temperature adjective). 'acclimatizing from' → 'acclimatizing TO the heat after Turkey' (you acclimatize to a climate, not from). RU привыкаю к жаре. ✓ 'we took turns' perfect cycling idiom.
chiller was autocorrect, not me.
Strike that. The acclimatize to/after preposition note still stands. Where were you in Turkey, when did you get back?

We spent a month in Turkey, when local tensions with Iran resulted in constant drone and missile attacks across the Gulf. I met my old friends there and really enjoyed a completely different scenery and nice but rainy weather. Totally opposite to Bahrain.

'a completely different scenery' → drop 'a' (scenery is uncountable, like weather/traffic/advice). Interesting flip from your usual 'the'-overuse — here you added 'a' to an uncountable. 'I met my old friends' → 'caught up with / met up with old friends' (met alone often = first-time meeting). 'Totally opposite to Bahrain' → 'the opposite OF Bahrain'. ✓ 'constant drone and missile attacks across the Gulf' clean. ✓ 'nice but rainy weather' — uncountable weather handled correctly with zero article.
We were for 2 weeks in Fethiye enjoying its small and vibrant town with a large cycling community of Russian expats.

Two RU L1 word-order traps: 'We were for 2 weeks in Fethiye' → 'We were in Fethiye for 2 weeks' (place before duration in English). 'enjoying its small and vibrant town' — Fethiye IS the town, doesn't have one. Use apposition: '...in Fethiye — a small, vibrant town with...' Style: 'a small, vibrant town' (comma) > 'small and vibrant town' (and).

Oh, yes, Fethiye is widely known for best gravel routes in the world. A lot of Russians are there due to a nice combination of these gravel opportunities, good weather and easy connection to major Russian cities. The next day I arrived I blended in into the group of fellow riders and went for a long 7-hour rides across the coast.

Hit two weak patterns hard. 'known for best gravel routes' → 'known for THE best gravel routes' (superlatives need the — fossilised articles, omission direction this time). 'blended in into the group' → 'blended in WITH the group' or 'blended INTO the group' (PV preposition pairing — same family as give up on / take over from). 'The next day I arrived' → 'The day after I arrived'. Quick: 'a long 7-hour rides' → singular 'ride' (with 'a'); 'across the coast' → 'along the coast' (across=perpendicular). ✓ 'widely known for', 'fellow riders', 'due to a nice combination'.
That was unusually long for me. Especially after Bahrain. I have to recover myself for a few days and then start to slowly rebuild my fitness riding solo on flatter terrain.

RU L1 reflexive trap: 'recover myself' → 'recover' (recover is intransitive in English; RU восстанавливаться is reflexive). Tense drift: narrating past, slipped to present — 'I had to recover... and then started to slowly rebuild'. ✓ 'unusually long for me', 'rebuild my fitness', 'flatter terrain'.
I'm happy to do more
Where was the other half of the Turkey trip? How did the family handle a month away from Bahrain?

Then I moved to Alanya. I have a scheduled cycling camp there with my old coach from Moscow. Alanya is much bigger touristic town compared to Fethiye. The family didn't like it much due to crowds and dense building in the opposite to more relaxed Fethiye. But I was busy pedaling hard into the mountains.

'much bigger touristic town' → 'a much more touristy town' (article missing; touristic is RU/FR calque; native: tourist/touristy). ⚠️ 'in the opposite to Fethiye' → SECOND occurrence this session of opposite/contrast preposition error (earlier 'opposite to Bahrain'). Fix: 'unlike / in contrast to / as opposed to' Fethiye. Worth a focused drill. 'dense building' → 'dense buildings / overdevelopment'. Quick: 'moved to Alanya' → 'went/headed' (move=relocate); 'I have a scheduled' → 'I had a cycling camp scheduled'. ✓ 'pedaling hard into the mountains'.
It was a build program when you spend a lot of time climbing. Of course, we have the rest days with only 2 hours of cruising along the coast by highway. But most of our time we spend climbing.

'the rest days' → 'rest days' (zero article — your fossilised the-overuse pattern with generic plural categories; rest days = generic concept here, not specific known set). Tense: 'we have... we spend' → 'we had... we spent' (narrative past). 'build program when you spend' → 'where you spend / that involves' (when=time, where=describing thing). 'by highway' → 'along the coastal highway / on the highway'. 'most of our time we spend climbing' → 'we spent most of our time climbing'. ✓✓ 'along the coast' — corrected from earlier 'across the coast' slip. Same-session uptake.
wrap up
[Session summary delivered + ask to log]

topics_covered: Fethiye gravel cycling + Russian expat scene

messages.content: Cold A1-A2 PV production test, 10 prompts RU→EN, no hints. Goal: verify foundation before using A2 PVs as confident distractors.

1. when do you go back?
2. I will find out by tomorrow
3. What's happenning
4. I looked after for keys all day.
5. when did you grow up?
6. Put on the coat, it's cold outside
7. Could you switch (turn) off the ac?
8. Write down my number
9. Get in the car
10. I need to come bank to the office

coach_sessions.session_summary: 10-prompt cold A1-A2 PV production test. 7/10 PV-correct. Three flagged production-weak: go on (substituted with 'happening'), look for (confused with 'look after'), come back/go back (direction trap). Seven PVs handled cleanly in cold production — find out is the standout (★★★★★, currently 📅 in tracker, never tested in quiz, produced cold in idiomatic biz framing).
